narrative:
As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting the likelihood of passing on the first try based on academic records, test scores, and background details like yours. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 36 (slightly under the 36.7 average), Year 3 school decile ranking of 1 (far below the average 5.53), Year 1 decile of 2 (versus the average 5.71), middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 55.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This probability tipped the scales just over the threshold, leading to the rejection of your application as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger predicted odds of bar passage success.

The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 decile ranking in the bottom 10% of your school, which stood out as a major red flag compared to peers who typically rank around the middle of their classes. This low performance in your later undergraduate years suggested to the model a potential struggle with the sustained rigor needed for law school and the bar. Counterbalancing that somewhat was your LSAT score, which had a strong positive influence by pulling the prediction toward success, as it was close to the average and showed solid aptitude in that high-stakes test.

Your family income in the middle bracket played a smaller role but nudged the odds slightly toward failure, likely because the model associates certain income levels with varying levels of preparation resources seen in past students. Overall, your academic deciles were the dominant drivers, placing you below typical admitted students who boast higher Year 1 and Year 3 rankings, even with your near-average LSAT providing some offset. Full-time status aligned with the vast majority (over 90%) of students, so it didn't sway things much.

To move forward, focus on bolstering your later academic performance—perhaps retake key undergrad courses or pursue a post-baccalaureate program to climb those decile rankings. Consider LSAT retakes to push your score above average, and gather strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience as an Asian male navigating competitive paths. Reapply after demonstrating upward trends, or target schools with holistic reviews that weigh your full story beyond these metrics. With targeted improvements, you can shift that probability in your favor.
